title: UniSuper, Hostplus lead super thrivers
full_text:
UniSuper, the country's fourth-biggest pension fund, is outpacing its nearest competitors with investment returns in its popular high-growth strategy nearing 12.8 per cent two weeks before the end of a volatile financial year dominated by the conflict in the Middle East and artificial intelligence.
Its closest rival is Hostplus, whose high-growth portfolio has climbed about 12.3 per cent in the past 12 months, according to an analysis of the superannuation funds' publicly disclosed unit prices.
AustralianSuper, HESTA and Rest Super have also pushed into double-digits with their respective high-growth plans, which are more exposed to the globally listed equity markets analysts say are the "key driver" for investment performance, as compared to default balanced funds.
"The higher the allocation to international shares, the better you would have done," said Chant West super investment head Mano Mohankumar.
"If you had a high percentage of your international shares hedged, it is even better. Because of the appreciation of the dollar over the last 12 months, converting those gains back would have eroded some of the strong returns."
There were still laggards - of the eight largest industry funds Aware Super's high-growth plan was up just 8.3 per cent as of June 16, and returns in Australian Retirement Trust's comparable strategy were about 9 per cent.
ART head of investment strategy Andrew Fisher said the fund, which manages assets of about $350 billion, had rotated investments into more private assets at the expense of stocks ahead of a potential correction.
"We're getting to like an end of a three-year cycle now," he said.
"What tends to happen is one of two things: if listed markets stay this far ahead of unlisted for a long period of time, unlisted will eventually catch up, so your discount rates will reprice or risk premiums compress. There will be a catch-up that way or listed markets will come back to earth."
The balanced options across the funds reviewed had, on average, returned 9.4 per cent at the time of the analysis. ART and Aware again lagged. Hostplus' balanced portfolio led with a 10.6 per cent return.
These returns compare with the 4.4 per cent gain on the S&P/ASX200 over the same period. Mohankumar said the default balanced portfolios of the super funds were increasingly becoming more exposed to international shares, which now made up about a third of their allocation.
The super funds, however, were still far behind the 21.1 per cent growth in the S&P500 and 29.9 per cent hike in the Nasdaq Composite index. Consistent volatility has become a feature of global markets, following the war between the United States and Iran and the resulting shock to oil supplies, and recurrent fears AI may not yet deliver the productivity benefits required to justify expenditure for key players such as Alphabet, Meta or Amazon.
Mohankumar said that markets had rebounded since February 28, when the conflict in the Middle East began. "Returns of around 9 per cent would have seemed unlikely in March and April," he said. "A final result around that mark would mean four straight years of strong returns for super funds."
Aware head of investment strategy Michael Winchester said most members were in the high-growth strategy, and that "if you asked me at the start of the financial year whether I would be happy with an 8 per cent return, I would have said, 'yep - we'll take that one with the bank'."
Winchester said Aware's active management strategy had it underweight US equities and technology - ground zero for the AI hype - but overweight sectors such as healthcare, dragging down the fund's broader returns.
On private markets, Winchester said commercial and retail property were looking more attractive after being "beaten down" over the past few years.
"In the infrastructure space, we're looking for opportunities in the energy transition, the digital infrastructure space, so data centres, fibre cables and so on - those assets are pretty appealing."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
